skill.name: wsdiscovery
skill.description: Discover and enumerate WS-Discovery devices, including ONVIF cameras, NVRs, printers, and IoT equipment on authorized networks.
skill.summary: Find WS-Discovery and ONVIF devices on an authorized network.
skill.author: BrownFineSecurity

security_audit.summary: The static external-command findings are partly true because the skill instructs an agent to run the wsdiscovery command against user-provided hosts. The hardcoded IP addresses are documentation examples, and the weak-cryptography alert at SKILL.md line 3 is a false positive with no matching cryptographic code. This is a legitimate but dual-use network discovery skill and should be published with an authorization warning.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Guided External Scanner Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill tells the AI agent to run wsdiscovery commands with a user-supplied hostname or IP address. This is expected for the tool, but it can initiate network probing if used without authorization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly instructs execution of wsdiscovery and shows several command invocations. The commands appear legitimate for a security scanner, but the external execution and user-provided target create real misuse risk.

medium_findings.title: Dual-Use Network Discovery Capability
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to discover and enumerate WS-Discovery, ONVIF, and IoT devices. This can expose IP addresses, device UUIDs, firmware versions, serial numbers, and service endpoints on networks where the user has access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The stated purpose and examples clearly perform network reconnaissance. The behavior is normal for authorized security work, but it can be abused for unauthorized device enumeration.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded IP Addresses Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The detected IP addresses appear only in example commands. No evidence found that the skill silently contacts those addresses or embeds a fixed external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are inside Markdown examples using private or multicast addresses. They do not show hidden beaconing, credential transfer, or a hardcoded command target.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Has No Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography alert points to the YAML description line. No evidence found of cryptographic algorithms, hashing functions, or encryption routines in that line.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md line 3 is plain metadata describing WS-Discovery usage. It contains no cryptographic implementation or algorithm selection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Command Examples With User-Controlled Targets
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides command examples that accept a hostname, IP address, or multicast address. Agents should require explicit authorization before running these commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is visible in multiple command examples and is central to the skill. The risk depends on user authorization and target ownership.

content.value_statement: Unknown WS-Discovery devices can create unmanaged exposure on local networks. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code run authorized discovery and explain device details.
actual_capabilities: Guides use of the wsdiscovery command for a hostname or IP address.
Supports text, JSON, quiet, and verbose output modes described by the skill.
Explains discovered IP addresses, ports, device UUIDs, and service endpoints.
Identifies ONVIF-related metadata such as manufacturer, model, serial number, and firmware version.
Suggests WS-Discovery multicast scanning for authorized device discovery.
limitations: Only finds devices that support and respond to WS-Discovery.
Requires network access to the target host or multicast segment.
Does not perform authentication, exploitation, or vulnerability validation.
Can produce sensitive inventory data and should be used only with authorization.

use_cases.title: Audit Network Cameras
use_cases.description: Find ONVIF cameras and review exposed device metadata during an approved site assessment.

use_cases.title: Inventory IoT Devices
use_cases.description: Discover WS-Discovery devices and collect basic identification details for asset records.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot Discovery Issues
use_cases.description: Compare standard and verbose discovery output when cameras or NVRs are missing from management tools.

prompt_templates.title: Discover One Host
prompt_templates.prompt: Use wsdiscovery to check this authorized host for WS-Discovery devices: [host]. Summarize any devices found.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner discovery on a single approved target.

prompt_templates.title: Review Device Metadata
prompt_templates.prompt: Run wsdiscovery against [host] and explain the device type, model, firmware, serial number, and service endpoints.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of discovered device identity.

prompt_templates.title: Collect Machine-Readable Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Use wsdiscovery with JSON output for [host]. Convert the result into a concise asset inventory summary.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate Discovery Failures
prompt_templates.prompt: Run verbose WS-Discovery against [host] and compare the full response with the expected ONVIF metadata fields.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced troubleshooting with verbose protocol output.

output_examples.input: Discover WS-Discovery devices on an authorized camera subnet.
output_examples.output: The scan found two ONVIF cameras. Both exposed management service endpoints, model names, firmware versions, and device UUIDs.

output_examples.input: Explain verbose output from one network camera.
output_examples.output: The response identifies the device as an ONVIF camera and lists manufacturer, hardware version, serial number, and metadata version.

output_examples.input: Create an asset summary from discovery results.
output: Service endpoints that need access review.

best_practices: Confirm authorization before scanning any host or multicast segment.
Start with one known device before scanning a wider discovery address.
Treat serial numbers, firmware versions, and service endpoints as sensitive inventory data.
anti_patterns: Do not scan public or third-party networks without written permission.
Do not assume missing results mean no devices exist on the network.
Do not publish raw device identifiers or service endpoints in public reports.

faq.question: What devices can this skill help find?
faq.answer: It helps find WS-Discovery devices, especially ONVIF cameras, NVRs, printers, and other IoT equipment.

faq.question: Does this skill exploit devices?
faq.answer: No. It supports discovery and enumeration only. It does not authenticate, exploit, or modify devices.

faq.question: Why is authorization important?
faq.answer: Discovery can reveal sensitive device inventory data. Only scan networks where you have permission.

faq.question: Can it produce JSON output?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill describes JSON output for machine-readable inventory and reporting workflows.

faq.question: Will it find every camera?
faq.answer: No. It only finds devices that support WS-Discovery and respond from the reachable network path.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
